beats.text: Simple exponential smoothing forecasts a level that wanders in noise by weighting recent observations more than old ones.

visual.sub: One parameter, one pass over the data

beats.text: Each new reading nudges the running level toward it by a fraction alpha, the smoothing learning rate.
With alpha of 0.3, a level of 10 and a new reading of 20 update to 13, moving thirty percent of the gap.
The error-correction form reads like online learning: each forecast miss pulls the level a fraction alpha toward fixing it.
Unrolled, the weight on each past point decays geometrically: 0.30, then 0.21, then 0.147, each step losing a factor of 0.7.
Small alpha means a long memory and slow fade; large alpha means a short memory that tracks the latest point.
Simple smoothing forecasts a flat line, so it lags on any series that is climbing or falling.
Holt added a trend term updated by its own parameter beta, so two recurrences run in lockstep.
The forecast is no longer flat but a straight line of slope b, projected h steps ahead.
A linear trend projected far out is usually too aggressive, since real series tend to flatten.
The damped trend bends the forecast toward a finite ceiling and is one of the most reliable automatic forecasters known.
The naming just counts recurrences: single tracks level, double adds trend, triple will add season.
Most operational series breathe on a calendar: weekly retail, daily electricity, monthly tourism.

visual.sub: A third recurrence for the calendar

beats.text: Winters added a vector of m seasonal indices, each updated by its own parameter gamma.
The additive form smooths the deseasonalised observation and adds the matching seasonal index back to the forecast.
Use additive when the swing is a constant size and multiplicative when the swing grows with the level.
With a level of 200 and a December multiplier of 1.4, the one-step forecast is 280, forty percent above the level.
The seasonal recurrence lets the fit hug peaks and troughs a flat level would slice straight through.
For forty years smoothing was a bag of recurrences with no probability model, so uncertainty could not be quantified.
Hyndman and colleagues showed each method is the point forecast of a state-space model with one source of error.
A single shock drives both the observation and every state update through the smoothing constants.
A three-letter code labels error, trend and season, giving thirty admissible models chosen by AIC.
The payoff is a likelihood to maximise, AIC for selection, and exact prediction intervals the old recurrences could not give.
Across the M3 and M4 competitions covering over a hundred thousand series, ETS variants stayed brutally hard to beat.
You do not set the smoothing parameters by hand; a fitting procedure picks them along with the initial states.

visual.sub: Minimising the sum of squared errors

beats.text: The standard objective minimises the in-sample sum of squared one-step forecast errors.
Because each forecast comes from the recurrences, the objective is nonlinear and solved by numerical optimisation.
More observation noise slides the optimal alpha left, while more genuine drift slides it right.
Do not minimise error on the same data you report accuracy on; hold out the tail or use rolling-origin evaluation.
When the curve goes flat the data does not pin alpha down, and any value in the basin forecasts about the same.
